inspection_steps: Pull cam cover and inspect both inner + outer CCT shoes for cracks, missing chunks, or glazed wear marks
Check cam chain deflection with feeler gauge — replace if slack exceeds 0.030 in. beyond stock
Pull magnetic drain plug and primary chaincase magnet, look for ferrous shavings from shoe wear
If shoes are glazed but intact, upgrade to S&S gear-drive conversion before the engine eats a cam bearing

symptoms: stator AC output drops with heat
inspection_steps: Measure AC output between stator leads at 1000 rpm — spec is 18-22 VAC; below 16 VAC replace
Check stator resistance lead-to-lead (0.1-0.3 ohm) and lead-to-ground (infinite)
Inspect stator wire loom where it exits the primary case for chafed insulation against inner chaincase
Upgrade to Cycle Electric CE-32A 32-amp stator if original keeps cooking under load

symptoms: uneven chain tension across swingarm travel
inspection_steps: Pull chain away from rear sprocket at 3 o'clock position — if teeth show more than half a link, chain is stretched
Inspect front sprocket teeth for shark-finning or asymmetric wear (tells you chain was run too tight or too loose)
Measure 20-pin span with caliper; if over 1% above factory spec, replace chain, both sprockets, and rubber dampers as a set
Never mix old chain with new sprocket or vice versa — always replace chain/countershaft/rear as a matched set

symptoms: soft front end dive under braking
inspection_steps: Wipe fork tube clean, ride 10 miles, inspect again — a fresh oily ring above the wiper confirms seal failure
Pull fork dust wiper and slip a Seal Mate tool under the seal to scrape embedded grit; often buys 6+ months before full rebuild
Check tube for nicks or pitting under the dust seal — a pitted tube will eat a new seal in weeks
Full service: drain, pull cartridge, replace seal + bushing + dust wiper, refill with spec'd viscosity fork oil to correct level

symptoms: rear end feels loose in corners
rear wheel play when rocked side to side
inspection_steps: Jack rear wheel, grab at 12 and 6 — any detectable play means bearing is shot; also spin and listen for dry grinding
Pull the rear wheel, remove seals, spin bearings by finger — should be silent glass-smooth; grit or notchy rotation = replace
Replace bearings as a PAIR per side (both bearings in a hub, both wheels if both show any play) and always renew the inner spacer
Pack new bearings with waterproof moly grease before install; factory pre-pack is usually insufficient for rainy-season riders
Drain oil, remove right engine case cover, inspect cast aluminum lever for stress cracks at the fulcrum and spring perch
If original factory lever is still in the engine and mileage > 15k, preemptively replace with Eagle Mike upgraded doohickey kit
Install upgraded torsion spring at the same time — the stock spring loses tension and compounds lever failure
Reset balancer chain timing per service manual before buttoning up — a timing error here self-destructs the balancer

symptoms: cold start rattle for first 5 seconds
rattle that fades after oil pressure builds
cct plunger extended past service limit
inspection_steps: Remove CCT from head, inspect plunger extension against service manual limit — if at max, tensioner is done
Pull valve cover, check cam chain for stretch by counting free-play links at the tensioner rail
Install APE manual cam chain tensioner (PN RT-600) — eliminates the Honda hydraulic failure mode permanently
After installing manual CCT, set cam chain tension by rotating engine to TDC compression and adjusting per APE instructions

symptoms: chatter when releasing clutch from stop
inspection_steps: Pull primary/clutch cover, inspect basket fingers where steel plate tabs ride — notches deeper than 0.5 mm cause judder
Dress minor grooving with a flat file; deep grooves require basket replacement, no exceptions
Check primary reduction gear backlash — excessive backlash amplifies basket wear and must be corrected first
Replace steel + friction plates as a set when basket is replaced; mixing old friction plates with a new basket re-wears it fast

symptoms: bars want to center themselves off-straight
front end wobble at low speed
inspection_steps: Lift front wheel, grab lower fork legs, pull straight up — any knock on re-seating means loose bearings or brinelling
With front raised and steering in the bike's normal range, rock bars slowly side to side feeling for detents at dead-center
Full fix: remove triple clamp, press out old races from head tube and lower stem, install tapered All Balls or stock replacement
Always pack new bearings with waterproof grease and torque stem nut to spec (typically 45-55 Nm, check service manual)
Pull primary cover and inspect stock compensator sprocket and spring pack — collapsed springs or visibly worn ramps mean replace
Upgrade to SE Screamin' Eagle compensator PN 40041-10 or aftermarket (BAKER, Rivera) — eliminates the stock OEM failure mode
Inspect primary chain adjuster shoe while in there; deeply grooved shoes often accompany a tired compensator
Torque compensator bolt to 150-170 ft-lb with red Loctite — this bolt backs off and destroys the primary if undertorqued

symptoms: slack in primary chain when checked through inspection plug
inspection_steps: Remove derby/inspection cover and measure primary chain deflection at the tensioner with factory gauge — max 0.625 in
If tensioner is factory manual, loosen lock nut and adjust per service manual (1998-2006); if automatic, check shoe wear level
Pull primary cover to inspect tensioner shoe for cracks or chunks missing — replace before metal-on-metal contact starts
On 2007+ auto-tensioners, a failed tensioner will shed plastic into the primary — flush chaincase after replacement

symptoms: idle drop when spraying carb cleaner on manifold
inspection_steps: Spray carb cleaner or propane around the intake-to-head joint while at idle — any idle rise confirms an air leak
Pull intake boots (or manifold on EFI) and inspect rubber for cracks where it flexes — replace whole boot, not just o-ring
On EFI bikes, re-seat or replace the manifold-to-head O-ring AND the throttle body mating seal; both can leak independently
After repair, recheck idle mixture screw (CV carbs) or run TPS/IAC reset procedure (EFI) — air-leak comp is locked in ECU otherwise
Verify charging at 3000 rpm with known-good DVM — 14.0-14.8V normal, over 15.2V is regulator failure mode
Check R/R heatsink for heat discoloration and loose mounting; a bad ground through the mount can spike output
On Shindengen-style units, test stator leads for balance — imbalanced legs will trip a weak R/R into overcharge
Replace with upgraded MOSFET R/R (SH775 from later bikes is a common swap) — runs cooler and fails-safe to undercharge

symptoms: steering wobble when applying front brake
inspection_steps: Measure rotor run-out with dial indicator at rotor OD — service limit typically 0.3 mm, replace if exceeded
Measure rotor thickness at 4 spots around circumference — taper of 0.05 mm indicates uneven pad deposit (not true warp)
If taper, bed pads properly (ten stops from 60 mph, no coming to full stop) before condemning rotor
Replace rotors in pairs when both wear/warp — uneven front rotors cause bar pull under braking
verified_by: EBC tech + Motorcyclist braking tech article

inspection_steps: Run a tank of Techron Concentrate Plus at double-dose (1 oz per gal) and ride aggressively 100 miles — cheapest first attempt
If no improvement, pull injectors and have them ultrasonic-cleaned and flow-tested (RC Engineering, Witch Hunter)
Check fuel pressure at the rail against spec — low pressure mimics clogged injectors and must be ruled out first
Replace fuel filter in-line or in-tank at the same service; a plugged filter will re-contaminate freshly cleaned injectors
verified_by: Witch Hunter tech notes + ADVrider EFI troubleshooting

symptoms: throttle response flat-spotted on one bank
inspection_steps: Warm engine fully, connect Morgan Carbtune or vacuum gauges to each intake port sync nipple
Adjust slave throttle bodies to match master body's vacuum reading at 1200 rpm within 10 mmHg across all cylinders
Sweep throttle to 3000 rpm and confirm readings stay in sync — drift at higher rpm indicates a cable/linkage issue not a sync issue
Always sync AFTER setting valve clearances (out-of-spec lash mimics a sync problem)
verified_by: Morgan Carbtune docs + shop service procedures

symptoms: intermittent start that works after rocking bike
inspection_steps: Pull starter and inspect idler gear teeth + starter pinion for chipped teeth — replace any damaged gear
Remove alternator rotor to access starter clutch sprag — inspect rollers/springs for galling or flat spots
Replace sprag clutch assembly as a unit; individual rollers can be sourced but alignment is critical
Always inspect the ring gear on the back of the flywheel at the same time — worn ring-gear teeth cause repeat slip

symptoms: jumps out of gear under load
notchy shift into one specific gear
inspection_steps: Split cases, pull transmission mainshaft + countershaft, inspect shift fork tips for mushrooming or bluing (heat damage)
Measure shift fork groove width on sliding gears with caliper against service-manual spec — wear here is rarer but real
Check shift drum detents for wear at the detent plate — a weak detent lets gears walk under load
Always replace shift fork AND the mated sliding gear — worn pair causes repeat failure even with new fork alone
verified_by: Clymer manuals + transmission rebuild forum threads

symptoms: loss of power on one cylinder
cam lobe shows scuffing on inspection
shiny metallic flake on drain plug
inspection_steps: Pull valve cover, rotate engine through one cycle, inspect each lobe surface with strong light — scuffing/galling is terminal
Check that oil has proper ZDDP level (1200+ ppm zinc); modern SN/SP oils are too low-zinc for flat-tappet cams
If wear is caught early, replace lobe-matching lifter AND cam together — NEVER reuse old lifters on a new cam
Re-break-in with high-zinc break-in oil (Lucas Hot Rod, Brad Penn Break-In) at 2000 rpm for 20 minutes no idling
verified_by: Summit Racing tech + Harley Davidson tech service bulletins

symptoms: loss of compression on one cylinder
soft pop through the exhaust at idle
inspection_steps: Warm engine, shut down, pull valve cover, measure lash on each valve at TDC compression per service manual sequence
Compare to spec — tight exhaust side is the killer; zero lash means valve cannot close and will burn a seat in hours
For shim-over-bucket heads, swap shims to bring lash into spec; for screw-and-locknut, back off adjuster and re-torque locknut
Re-check lash after 500-1000 miles — new seats bed in and often tighten; ignoring this burns the valve you just fixed
Run a compression test cold, then wet (oil down the cylinder), then hot — 15%+ rise wet-vs-dry means rings, not valves
Leakdown test: pressurize cylinder at TDC, listen for hiss — hiss at oil fill = rings; at intake = intake valve; at exhaust = exhaust valve
Pull head, inspect cylinder bore for wear ridge at top of ring travel — a nail-catching ridge means bore is worn past service
Rebore to next oversize and fit matching oversized piston + fresh ring set; always plateau-hone for proper ring seating

symptoms: oil residue on underside of frame
inspection_steps: Clean area, run bike, re-inspect — identifying the actual seal source is 80% of the job; gravity can disguise origin
For output-side: pull front sprocket, snap out old seal with pick, drive new one in flush with proper seal driver (not a socket)
Inspect output shaft sealing surface for grooving — any groove deeper than 0.1 mm needs a Speedi-Sleeve or shaft replacement
Always replace the seal during clutch/primary service; the labor is zero-added and the seal is $10-$30
verified_by: universal service-manual procedure + ADVrider garage threads

symptoms: tick that goes away at high rpm
black sooty mark on header flange
popping on decel that wasn't there before
inspection_steps: With engine cold, run at idle and feel around header with a shop rag — pulse of hot air or soot line betrays the leak
Inspect collector welds and slip-joint at mid-pipe; aftermarket pipes crack at bends from cold-cycling fatigue
For hairline cracks at welds, a shop with TIG and stainless filler can re-weld; for split collectors, replace the pipe
Check exhaust gasket at head flange when tracing header leaks — gaskets harden and crumble, especially on air-cooled V-twins
verified_by: exhaust fabricator forums + Cycle World tech

symptoms: clutch engagement at end of lever travel
inspection_steps: Set free-play at the perch to factory spec (typically 2-4 mm); if adjusters are maxed out, cable is stretched and due
Lube cable with cable luber + motor oil or proper cable lube — dry cables feel stretched when they're actually binding
Inspect cable ends for frayed strands at the ferrules — any broken strand = replace whole cable immediately
When replacing, route new cable matching original path through frame; wrong routing causes early re-stretch
Pull bar-end weights and inspect internal rubber isolators — collapsed rubber is the #1 cause of new-vibration complaints
Torque bar-end bolt to spec with blue Loctite; undertorqued weight rattles and loses isolation effectiveness
If factory rubber is gone, aftermarket heavier weights (ProTaper, Rizoma) reduce buzz significantly — 40-50% mass helps
Check that grips themselves aren't bonded directly to bar without glue — direct bond transmits more vibration than clean grip glue
verified_by: Cycle World ergonomics + forum tribal knowledge

symptoms: chain tension varies as suspension cycles
swingarm has lateral play when pulled side to side
hinge feeling in rear end on throttle application
inspection_steps: Put bike on center stand or lift, grab swingarm near axle, push/pull laterally — any detectable play = pivot bearings worn
Pull swingarm, inspect needle/tapered bearings for brinelling or dry rust — repack with waterproof grease if still in service
Check pivot shaft for wear groove at the bearing contact patch; a grooved shaft will eat a new bearing in weeks
Torque pivot shaft nut to service-manual spec; over-torquing binds bearings, under-torquing lets them hammer themselves out

symptoms: oil sprayed onto swingarm by chain
inspection_steps: Remove sprocket cover, clean countershaft area, run bike — oil forming behind the sprocket confirms countershaft seal failure
Pull front sprocket (lock rear brake + breaker bar), pry old seal out with pick, drive new seal in square with seal driver
Inspect countershaft sealing surface for groove under old seal lip — a grooved shaft needs Speedi-Sleeve or shaft replacement
Fill transmission to spec level after seal replacement — leak was probably dropping level while you weren't watching
verified_by: universal service-manual procedure + sportbike shop wisdom

component: final drive / rear hub splines (shaft / belt hubs)
symptoms: shaft drive lash beyond service limit
inspection_steps: Pull rear wheel, clean spline grease off drive hub, inspect male + female splines for worn or rounded teeth
Measure drive lash at sprocket/pulley — over service-manual spec indicates worn splines or bearing pre-load loss
Repack splines with moly-60 anti-seize (NOT regular chassis grease — heat cooks it out in a month) at each tire change
Replace hub + matching drive if spline tips are rolled or teeth chipped; running it grenades the rear-drive unit
verified_by: BMW / Harley shaft-drive forums + Moto Guzzi service procedures

inspection_steps: Swap in a known-good bulb first — a single blown bulb is the #1 cause of hyper-flash and 'won't flash' symptoms
Measure battery voltage at flasher pin — below 12.2V causes thermal-style flashers to malfunction intermittently
If an LED conversion was done recently, install an LED-compatible electronic flasher OR add load resistors to each turn signal
Pull the flasher relay and test on the bench with a 12V source + bulb; a dead relay will not click and must be replaced
verified_by: universal electrical troubleshooting + Custom Dynamics LED install guide

symptoms: coolant drip from water pump weep hole
coolant loss with no visible leak
sweet smell near engine when warm
inspection_steps: Locate water pump weep hole on pump housing underside — a wet or crusty weep hole is an unambiguous seal failure indicator
Check coolant reservoir level and top-tank level weekly; ongoing loss confirms seal (not just a one-time spill)
Replace water pump as an assembly (not just seal + impeller) unless you have precision bearings-and-seals tools and know-how
Flush + refill with OEM-spec coolant and bleed per service manual; an air pocket in the pump kills a fresh seal fast
verified_by: liquid-cooled bike service manuals + shop repair experience
